categories.label: Power
categories.subtitle: Regency, weak kings and the collapse of royal authority

entries.label: Regency of Philippe d'Orléans — and Law's System
entries.hint: Louis XV is five years old — the Regent Philippe d'Orléans rules from Paris (not Versailles), relaxes court formality, and allows John Law's financial experiment; Law's System crashes in 1720, devastating French finances and creating lasting suspicion of paper money.
entries.note: Regency council records: Archives nationales, série E. John Law's Mississippi Company prospectus (1719): BnF. The crash of 1720: documented in contemporary pamphlets (BnF), police reports (Archives nationales Y). Faure (1977), La Banqueroute de Law: the standard study. The contrast between Regency libertinism and Versailles formality: Saint-Simon's Mémoires (he was present).

entries.label: Louis XV — le Bien-Aimé to the unloved
entries.hint: Louis XV reigns 59 years — initially popular ('le Bien-Aimé'), he loses the Seven Years War (1756–1763), giving up Canada and most of France's empire; his private correspondence with diplomats (the King's Secret) reveals parallel foreign policy conducted without his ministers' knowledge.
entries.note: Louis XV's private diplomatic correspondence ('le Secret du Roi'): Archives des Affaires Étrangères, Mémoires et Documents France 1397–2053. Published: Broglie (1878), Le Secret du Roi. The Seven Years War archives: Archives de la Guerre A1. McManners (1998), Church and Society, Vol.II for the religious dimension. Petitfils (2014), Louis XV: the standard modern biography.

entries.label: Louis XVI — the king who lost the initiative
entries.hint: Louis XVI inherits a fiscal crisis and an Enlightenment culture that questions royal authority — he appoints Turgot, Necker and other reformers, then dismisses them under court pressure; his convocation of the Estates-General in May 1789 inadvertently triggers the Revolution.
entries.note: Louis XVI's diary (covering 1766–1792): Archives nationales, KK 622 — the famous entry for 14 July 1789: 'Rien' (nothing, referring to his hunting). His appointment of Turgot 1774: Archives nationales. The Estates-General convocation decree 24 Jan 1789: Archives nationales, série AD. Price (2002), Preserving the Monarchy: the standard study of Louis XVI's political decisions.

entries.label: Assembly of Notables (1787) — the point of no return
entries.hint: Louis XVI and Calonne call the Assembly of Notables to approve a universal land tax (taxing all landowners including nobles and clergy) — the Notables refuse; Calonne is dismissed; Loménie de Brienne fails similarly; Louis XVI is forced to recall the Estates-General; this refusal by the privileged orders to accept fiscal reform is the constitutional point of no return before 1789.
entries.note: The Assembly proceedings (22 Feb–25 May 1787): Archives nationales, série KK. Calonne's reform proposal: Archives nationales. Harris (1979), Necker: Reform Statesman of the Ancien Régime: the standard study. Hardman (1995), French Politics 1774–1789, ch.9: the standard analysis of the Assembly's refusal. The connection to 1789: the convocation of the Estates-General 24 Jan 1789 is directly documented as a consequence of the Notables' failure.

entries.label: Maupeou Revolution (1771–1774) — the coup that could have saved the monarchy
entries.hint: Chancellor Maupeou strips the hereditary Parlements of their political power in January 1771 — exiling the judges and replacing them with crown-appointed magistrates; this is the most radical royal consolidation since Richelieu; Louis XVI reverses it immediately on accession (1774), restoring the Parlements' veto power and guaranteeing the fiscal gridlock of the 1780s.
entries.note: Maupeou's edict January 1771: Archives nationales, série E. The exile of the Parlement judges: documented. Louis XVI's reversal (12 Nov 1774): Gaceta — documented. Doyle (1970), 'The Parlements of France and the Breakdown of the Old Regime', French Historical Studies: the standard analysis. Swann (1995), Politics and the Parlement of Paris under Louis XV: the standard study of Maupeou's coup. The reversal's consequences: explicitly linked to 1788 by Necker, Malesherbes and other contemporaries — documented.

categories.label: Conflict
categories.subtitle: Seven Years War and the loss of empire

entries.label: War of Austrian Succession — Fontenoy and the 'stupid peace'
entries.hint: France wins the War of Austrian Succession including Marshal Saxe's brilliant victory at Fontenoy (11 May 1745) — but at the Treaty of Aix-la-Chapelle (1748) Louis XV returns all French territorial gains for no compensation; the popular phrase 'bête comme la paix' (as stupid as the peace) permanently damages his reputation as 'le Bien-Aimé'.
entries.note: The Battle of Fontenoy 11 May 1745: Archives de la Guerre A1 — Saxe's dispatches. Treaty of Aix-la-Chapelle (18 Oct 1748): Archives des Affaires Étrangères. The phrase 'bête comme la paix': documented in contemporary pamphlets (BnF). Showalter (2012), The Wars of Frederick the Great: contextualises Fontenoy. The Treaty's impact on Louis XV's reputation: documented in police surveillance reports of public opinion (Archives nationales, série Y) — Arlette Farge's work on these reports.

entries.label: Seven Years War — France loses its empire
entries.hint: The Seven Years War is France's catastrophic colonial defeat — Canada, most of India and Caribbean islands are lost to Britain; the Peace of Paris (1763) is the greatest colonial contraction in French history to that point; Voltaire mocks Canada as 'quelques arpents de neige'.
entries.note: Archives de la Guerre A1: French military records. Peace of Paris 10 Feb 1763: Archives des Affaires Étrangères; British National Archives SP 108. The battle of the Plains of Abraham 13 Sep 1759: documented in both French and British military records. Voltaire's 'quelques arpents de neige': Candide (1759), Ch.23. Dziembowski (1998), Un nouveau patriotisme français: analyses French public opinion during the war.

entries.label: American War of Independence — France's revenge
entries.hint: France enters the American war against Britain in 1778 — Lafayette volunteers, Rochambeau commands French forces at Yorktown; the Treaty of Paris (1783) partially reverses the 1763 losses but at enormous financial cost that accelerates the fiscal crisis leading to the Revolution.
entries.note: French declaration of war on Britain 17 Jun 1778: Archives des Affaires Étrangères. Rochambeau's Yorktown dispatches: Archives de la Guerre A1. Lafayette's letters: Library of Congress. French debt from the war: documented in Necker's Compte rendu (1781). Dull (1975), The French Navy and American Independence: the standard study.

categories.label: Order
categories.subtitle: Fiscal crisis, law reform and the collapse of absolutism

entries.label: The Calas Affair — Voltaire and judicial reform
entries.hint: Jean Calas, a Protestant merchant, is judicially tortured and executed in Toulouse on false evidence of killing his son to prevent conversion — Voltaire campaigns publicly for his rehabilitation; the case is overturned in 1765 and becomes the founding moment of European human rights advocacy.
entries.note: The Toulouse tribunal records: Archives départementales de la Haute-Garonne. The rehabilitation decision 1765: Archives nationales, Conseil du Roi. Voltaire, Traité sur la Tolérance (1763): BnF first edition. Bien (1960), The Calas Affair: the standard study. Voltaire's campaign letters: published in his Correspondence (Besterman edition, 51,000 letters — the largest surviving 18th-c correspondence).

entries.label: Necker's Compte rendu — public finance made public
entries.hint: Jacques Necker publishes the first public account of royal finances — the Compte rendu au roi (1781) is the first time French fiscal data is shared with the public; it sells 100,000 copies immediately and transforms public debate about royal spending.
entries.note: Compte rendu au roi (Feb 1781): BnF first edition. 100,000 copies in weeks: documented in publisher's records. Hardman (1995), French Politics 1774–1789: the standard study. Necker's subsequent dismissal and rehiring: documented in Archives nationales. The Compte rendu's reliability: debated by historians — Harris (1979), Necker: Reform Statesman of the Ancien Régime.

entries.label: Edict of Versailles (1787) — Protestants restored to civil existence
entries.hint: Louis XVI's Edict of Versailles (November 1787) officially restores civil rights to French Protestants — the right to marry legally, register births and deaths, and inherit property; ending over a century of legal non-existence following the Revocation of the Edict of Nantes (1685); a landmark of late Ancien Régime religious toleration.
entries.note: Édit de Versailles (November 1787): Archives nationales; Gaceta — text published. Lüthy (1959), La Banque protestante en France de la Révocation à la Révolution: the standard study of Protestant civil disability and its end. The practical consequences: Protestant marriage registers begin in 1788 — documented in departmental archives. The Calas Affair connection: Voltaire's campaign (1762–1765) documented as one of the long-term causes of this edict.
keywords: Louis XVI Edict of Versailles November
Revocation of the Edict of Nantes

categories.label: Belief
categories.subtitle: Enlightenment critique of religion and Jansenist resistance

entries.label: Convulsionnaires of Saint-Médard — 'God is forbidden from working miracles here'
entries.hint: Jansenist pilgrims flock to the tomb of Deacon Pâris at Saint-Médard cemetery (1728–1732) — falling into violent miraculous trances (convulsions); the crown closes the cemetery by force in January 1732; the famous satirical graffito follows: 'By order of the King, God is forbidden from working miracles in this place'; the episode becomes a focal point for anti-clerical and anti-royal mockery.
entries.note: The closure order 27 Jan 1732: Archives nationales. The graffito: documented in multiple contemporary sources and police reports. Kreiser (1978), Miracles, Convulsions and Ecclesiastical Politics in Early Eighteenth-Century Paris: the standard study. The police surveillance reports on the Convulsionnaires: Archives nationales, série Y (Arlette Farge has documented these). The episode's political significance: Voltaire uses it in his anti-clerical writings.

entries.label: Expulsion of the Jesuits from France — 1762
entries.hint: The Paris Parlement expels the Jesuits from France on 6 August 1762 — 3,000 Jesuits are forced to leave or secularise; it is the most dramatic assertion of Gallican parliamentary power before the Revolution.
entries.note: Parlement of Paris arrêt 6 Aug 1762: Archives nationales, série X. The Jesuits' response and appeal to the Pope: Vatican Archives (ASV). The royal edict confirming expulsion 1764: Archives nationales. Latreille (1950), L'Église catholique et la Révolution française, Vol.I: the standard study. Spain's parallel expulsion 1767: the two are connected — documented in both French and Spanish archives.

categories.label: Expression
categories.subtitle: The Rococo, Watteau, the salons and pre-revolutionary culture

entries.label: Watteau and the fête galante — Rococo as style
entries.hint: Jean-Antoine Watteau is admitted to the Académie Royale in 1717 with a new genre — the fête galante (elegant outdoor party); his Pilgrimage to the Island of Cythera defines the Rococo aesthetic; he dies of tuberculosis aged 36, leaving only 200 paintings.
entries.note: Pilgrimage to the Island of Cythera (1717): Louvre (INV 8525). Watteau's Académie reception recorded in: Procès-verbaux de l'Académie Royale (Archives nationales, série O1). The catalogue of his work: Rosenberg & Prat (1996), Antoine Watteau: catalogue raisonné des dessins. His death 18 Jul 1721: parish records Nogent-sur-Marne.

entries.label: Rameau and the Querelle des Bouffons
entries.hint: Jean-Philippe Rameau's Hippolyte et Aricie (1733) launches French Baroque opera's greatest period — the Querelle des Bouffons (1752–1754) pits Italian against French musical style, with Rousseau championing Italian and Rameau defending French; the debate is a proxy for the Enlightenment's cultural politics.
entries.note: Hippolyte et Aricie first performance 1 Oct 1733: Opéra records (BnF département de la musique). Rameau's autograph scores: BnF. The Querelle des Bouffons pamphlets (1752–1754): BnF — over 60 pamphlets. Rousseau's Lettre sur la musique françoise (1753): BnF first edition. Anthony (1973), French Baroque Music: the standard study.

entries.label: Place de la Concorde and Soufflot's Panthéon — Neoclassicism
entries.hint: Gabriel's Place de la Concorde (1755) and Soufflot's Panthéon (begun 1758, completed 1790) represent the neoclassical turn away from Rococo — both built under Louis XV and XVI; the Panthéon becomes the secular temple of the Revolution within months of completion.
entries.note: Place de la Concorde: extant. Building accounts: Archives nationales, série O1. Soufflot's Panthéon building accounts: Archives nationales. The conversion to secular use Apr 1791: documented in the Revolutionary assembly records. Middleton & Watkin (1987), Neoclassical and Nineteenth Century Architecture: the standard survey.

entries.label: David — Oath of the Horatii (1784)
entries.hint: Jacques-Louis David's Oath of the Horatii (1784) is the political manifesto of Neoclassicism — three sons swear to die for Rome, their women weep; its austere patriotism, civic duty and self-sacrifice become the visual ideology of the coming Revolution; Napoleon later calls it 'the Revolution already in action' (the phrase usually attributed to Beaumarchais's play).
entries.note: Oath of the Horatii (1784): Louvre (INV 3692). Exhibited at the Salon of 1785 (painted in Rome 1784): Salon catalogue — BnF. Johnson (1993), Jacques-Louis David: Art in Metamorphosis: the standard study. The painting's political reception: documented in contemporary press (Journal de Paris). David received 6,000 livres from the royal Direction des bâtiments — the royal commission documented in Archives nationales, série O1.
keywords: Jacques-Louis David Oath of the Horatii

entries.label: Beaumarchais — The Marriage of Figaro (1784)
entries.hint: Beaumarchais's Le Mariage de Figaro is banned by Louis XVI who calls it 'subversive'; its eventual performance on 27 April 1784 is a cultural explosion; the play's biting satire of noble privilege and incompetence is a primary literary assault on the Ancien Régime; Napoleon calls it 'the Revolution already in action'.
entries.note: First performance 27 Apr 1784: Comédie-Française records. Louis XVI's ban: documented in court records — he said it 'would be necessary to destroy the Bastille' before allowing it. The first edition: Paris, Ruault, 1785 — BnF. Lever (2009), Beaumarchais: the standard biography. Napoleon's phrase: documented in Las Cases, Mémorial de Sainte-Hélène. The play's 68 consecutive performances (a record): documented in Comédie-Française registers.

categories.label: Thought
categories.subtitle: Voltaire, Rousseau, the Encyclopédie and the republic of letters

entries.label: Montesquieu — De l'Esprit des Lois (1748)
entries.hint: Montesquieu's Spirit of the Laws (1748) distinguishes despotism, monarchy and republic; introduces the concept of separation of powers; directly influences the American and French constitutions; put on the Index of Forbidden Books by the Church.
entries.note: First edition: Barillot, Geneva, 1748 (2 vols): BnF. The Spirit of the Laws's influence on the US Constitution: Madison's Federalist No.47 quotes it explicitly. On the Index: documented in Vatican records. Shackleton (1961), Montesquieu: A Critical Biography: the standard study. The manuscript: Bibliothèque de Bordeaux.

entries.label: Diderot and d'Alembert's Encyclopédie
entries.loc: Paris (published by Le Breton and partners)
entries.hint: The Encyclopédie ou Dictionnaire raisonné (28 volumes, 1751–1772) is the collective mind of the Enlightenment — 74,000 articles by 142 contributors including Voltaire, Rousseau and Montesquieu; it is banned twice and circulates clandestinely; the primary intellectual monument of the 18th century.
entries.note: First edition: Paris, Le Breton, 1751–1772 (17 text vols + 11 plates vols): BnF holds complete sets. The ban of 1759: Archives nationales. The ARTFL Encyclopédie Project (University of Chicago): digitised full text. Wilson (1972), Diderot: the standard English biography. The contributor list and article attributions: documented in the project's scholarly apparatus.

entries.label: Physiocracy — Quesnay's Tableau Économique (1758)
entries.hint: François Quesnay's Tableau Économique (1758) is the first systematic diagram of economic flows — founding Physiocracy, which argues agricultural land is the sole source of real wealth and advocates laissez-faire economics; it directly influences Turgot and Adam Smith; the Physiocrats are the first self-conscious 'school' of economic thought in European history.
entries.note: Tableau Économique (first version 1758, printed at Versailles by Louis XV himself — 3 copies only): BnF holds a copy. Quesnay's Encyclopédie articles (1756–1757): his earlier economic thought. Meek (1962), The Economics of Physiocracy: the standard English study. Adam Smith's acknowledgment of Quesnay's influence: Wealth of Nations Book IV ch.9. Turgot's Réflexions sur la formation et la distribution des richesses (1766): the direct Physiocratic policy application.

entries.label: Rousseau — Du Contrat Social (1762)
entries.hint: Rousseau's Social Contract opens with 'Man is born free, and everywhere he is in chains' — the founding text of modern democratic theory; banned in France and Geneva immediately; its concept of the 'general will' directly shapes the French Revolution's ideology.
entries.note: Du Contrat Social: Marc-Michel Rey, Amsterdam, 1762; BnF. Banned by Paris Parlement 11 Jun 1762 (same session as Émile): Archives nationales, série X. The manuscript: Bibliothèque de Neuchâtel. Masters (1968), The Political Philosophy of Rousseau: the standard analytical study. The Social Contract's influence on the Revolution: Schama (1989), Citizens, throughout.

entries.label: Voltaire's Philosophical Dictionary (1764)
entries.hint: Voltaire publishes the Dictionnaire philosophique portatif (1764) — 118 articles attacking religious intolerance, superstition and arbitrary power in alphabetical form; small enough to hide in a pocket ('portatif'); the most widely distributed Enlightenment text after the Encyclopédie.
entries.note: First edition: Geneva, 1764; BnF. Voltaire's 51,000-letter correspondence: Besterman edition (Geneva 1953–65) — the largest surviving 18th-century correspondence corpus. Voltaire's papers: BnF and Institut et Musée Voltaire, Geneva. Gay (1959), Voltaire's Politics: the standard study. The Dictionnaire's multiple revised editions (Voltaire kept updating it): documented in bibliographic records.

categories.label: Economy
categories.subtitle: Fiscal crisis, colonial wealth and the debt that caused the Revolution

entries.label: The royal debt crisis — anatomy of fiscal collapse
entries.hint: France spends the 18th century in escalating fiscal crisis — Law's crash (1720), seven wars, and Versailles maintenance; by 1788 debt service consumes 62% of state revenue; Louis XVI's inability to tax the nobility or clergy makes bankruptcy inevitable.
entries.note: Necker's Compte rendu (1781): the primary public document. Calonne's reform proposals 1786: Archives nationales, série E. The Assembly of Notables rejection 1787: Archives nationales. Hardman (1995), French Politics 1774–1789: synthesises the fiscal evidence. Bairoch (1997), Victoires et déboires: comparative European fiscal data.

entries.label: Eden Treaty (1786) — British textiles flood France
entries.hint: The Eden Treaty (Anglo-French free trade, 26 September 1786) lowers tariffs between Britain and France — cheap machine-made British textiles flood the French market, devastating the textile industries of Rouen and Lyon; mass urban unemployment results just before the harvest failures of 1788; the treaty is a direct economic catalyst for the social crisis preceding the Revolution.
entries.note: Eden Treaty 26 Sep 1786: Archives des Affaires Étrangères; British National Archives. The textile impact: documented in the Rouen and Lyon Chambres de Commerce reports (Archives départementales). Crouzet (1964), 'Wars, Blockade and Economic Change in Europe 1792–1815', Journal of Economic History: contextualises the treaty. Henderson (1957), 'The Anglo-French Commercial Treaty of 1786', Economic History Review: the standard analysis. The unemployment figures: documented in pre-revolutionary police reports (Archives nationales, série F12).

categories.label: Exchange
categories.subtitle: The Republic of Letters, salonnières and intellectual networks

entries.label: The salonnières — women as the infrastructure of the Enlightenment
entries.loc: Paris (salons of Tencin, du Deffand, Lespinasse, Necker, Geoffrin)
entries.hint: The French Enlightenment is organised through salons hosted by women — Marie Thérèse Rodet Geoffrin finances the Encyclopédie; Julie de Lespinasse manages d'Alembert's correspondence; Suzanne Necker's salon launches her husband's political career; the salonnières are the primary intellectual infrastructure of the period.
entries.note: Madame Geoffrin's correspondence: Bibliothèque de l'Institut de France. Her financial support of the Encyclopédie: documented in Diderot's correspondence. Julie de Lespinasse's letters: BnF (published 1809). Madame du Deffand's 800-letter correspondence with Horace Walpole: Lewis (1939–1980), Yale Walpole Correspondence. Goodman (1994), The Republic of Letters: A Cultural History of the French Enlightenment: the standard study.

entries.label: Saint-Domingue — the most profitable colony in history
entries.hint: By the 1780s Saint-Domingue (Haiti) produces 40% of Europe's sugar and 60% of its coffee — on the labour of 500,000 enslaved Africans; it is the most profitable colony in the Atlantic world; the enslaved population outnumbers the free population 10 to 1.
entries.note: Saint-Domingue plantation records: ANOM (Aix-en-Provence). Population statistics: Census of Saint-Domingue 1789 (500,000 enslaved, 32,000 white colonists, 28,000 free people of colour): Archives nationales, Colonies G1. Dubois (2004), Avengers of the New World: analyses the data. The colony's trade value: documented in Chambre de Commerce de Marseille records.

entries.label: The Diplomatic Revolution of 1756
entries.hint: France reverses its century-long alliance with Prussia and makes peace with Austria (Treaty of Versailles, 1 May 1756) — the 'Diplomatic Revolution' reorganises European alliances before the Seven Years War; it is the most dramatic single diplomatic shift of the 18th century.
entries.note: Treaty of Versailles 1 May 1756: Archives des Affaires Étrangères (La Courneuve). Kaunitz's role in negotiating the reversal: Haus-, Hof- und Staatsarchiv, Vienna. Scott (1990), The Emergence of the Eastern Powers 1756–1775: the standard study. The reversal's irony — France now defending the Habsburgs — noted by all contemporary observers including Frederick the Great.

entries.label: First Partition of Poland (1772) — France's geopolitical demotion
entries.hint: Prussia, Russia and Austria partition Poland between them without France being able to intervene — demonstrating France's demotion from 'arbiter of Europe' to passive spectator; Poland was a traditional French client state; the partition reveals the shift of European power toward the eastern monarchies and deeply bruises French national prestige under Louis XV.
entries.note: Partition treaty 5 Aug 1772: documented in the state archives of all three powers. France's inability to respond: documented in diplomatic correspondence (Archives des Affaires Étrangères). Lord (1915), The Second Partition of Poland: the standard study with primary source analysis. Scott (1990), The Emergence of the Eastern Powers 1756–1775: the standard analysis of France's geopolitical demotion. The connection to the Diplomatic Revolution of 1756: the Austrian alliance that was supposed to solve this problem demonstrably failed.

categories.label: Lives
categories.subtitle: Gender, sexuality & marginalised experience · evidence anchored

entries.label: Saint-Domingue — the largest enslaved population in the Caribbean
entries.hint: 500,000 enslaved Africans in Saint-Domingue by 1789 — the largest enslaved population in any Caribbean colony; the plantation records document individual lives in the most complete surviving colonial slavery archive; their resistance is also documented.
entries.note: ANOM plantation records: Aix-en-Provence — the primary archive for individual enslaved people's names and histories. The maroon communities: documented in colonial police records (ANOM). Geggus (2002), Haitian Revolutionary Studies: uses the ANOM archive. The Haitian Revolution (1791) begins in the folio that follows — the connection is direct: the enslaved population documented here is the one that revolts.

entries.label: Enfants Trouvés — one-third of Parisian births abandoned
entries.loc: Paris (Hôpital des Enfants-Trouvés; now part of Necker Hospital)
entries.hint: By the 1770s, nearly one-third of all children born in Paris are abandoned at the Hôpital des Enfants-Trouvés — where first-year mortality exceeds 80%; extreme urban poverty and illegitimacy drive the crisis; Rousseau himself abandons his five children to this hospital (documented); the institution is the structural evidence for the Ancien Régime's failure to provide for its poorest.
entries.note: Enfants-Trouvés admission registers: Archives de l'Assistance Publique — Hôpitaux de Paris (APHP). The abandonment statistics: documented in Dupâquier (1988), Histoire de la population française Vol.II. The 80%+ first-year mortality rate: documented in Hufton (1974), The Poor of Eighteenth-Century France: the standard study. Rousseau's abandonment of his five children: documented in his own Confessions (1782–1789) — a primary source for the practice's social normalisation. The Enfants-Trouvés: the single most damning primary source for Ancien Régime social failure.

entries.label: Madame de Pompadour — political power through the king's favour
entries.hint: Jeanne-Antoinette Poisson (Madame de Pompadour) is Louis XV's official mistress from 1745 — she appoints and dismisses ministers, directs foreign policy, patronises Voltaire and the philosophes, and commissions the Sèvres porcelain factory; her correspondence is a primary source for political decision-making.
entries.note: Pompadour's correspondence: BnF fonds français. Her role in the Diplomatic Revolution 1756: argued by Lever (2003), Madame de Pompadour using Affaires Étrangères archives. The Sèvres commission: Manufacture nationale de Sèvres archives. Her apartments at Versailles: Château de Versailles archives. She outlasted all rival mistresses (1745–1764) through political skill documented in multiple sources.

entries.label: François Mackandal and marronage — enslaved resistance in Saint-Domingue
entries.hint: François Mackandal, an enslaved African in Saint-Domingue, leads a major poisoning conspiracy (1751–1757) targeting white colonists and their animals — documented in the plantation and court records; he organises the maroon (escaped slave) communities in the mountains; executed by burning in January 1758; the first documented large-scale enslaved resistance in Saint-Domingue before the 1791 revolution.
entries.note: Mackandal's trial and execution January 1758: Archives nationales, Colonies F3 — the primary judicial documentation. The maroon communities: documented in colonial police records (ANOM). Geggus (2002), Haitian Revolutionary Studies, ch.3: uses the ANOM archive. Fick (1990), The Making of Haiti: the standard modern study. Moreau de Saint-Méry's description of the execution: published in his Description de la partie française de l'Isle Saint-Domingue (1797) — an eyewitness account.

events.l: First Partition of Poland — France sidelined
Enfants Trouvés — one-third of Parisian births abandoned
